text: The surgeon performs cystoscopy, introducing a cystoscope into the urethra. An infrared ureteric catheter is passed into the right ureter, guided by a wire, and inserted to a depth of fifteen centimeters, marked every five centimeters. The catheter is then removed and similarly introduced into the left ureter, ensuring minimal bladder distension to maintain visibility of the ureteric regions.

The surgeon sets the camera to iris mode for optimal visualization through the supra-umbilical port. The camera port is positioned according to the baseball diamond concept, with the first arc at eighteen centimeters and the second arc at twenty-four centimeters from the target. The right-hand port is prepared for ligature or harmonic use, while the left-hand port is designated for graspers. The contralateral port is managed by the assistant.

The surgeon makes a small stab wound at the camera point and lifts the abdominal wall with a Veress needle. After inserting the needle, irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests are performed to ensure correct placement. The insufflator is started using a Striker pneumoflator, with the preset pressure set to fifteen. As the actual pressure increases to nine, the flow rate is adjusted to three liters per minute for complete distension. The incision is enlarged to eleven mm, and an optical trocar is carefully introduced.

The surgeon introduces an 11 mm trocar using a screwing motion, followed by the insertion of a telescope. A 5 mm working port is placed on the right side, and another port is positioned 7.5 cm laterally. Diagnostic laparoscopy confirms the uterus is intact. The Mangeshkar uterine manipulator with a 3.5 cm colpotomizer is introduced, encircling the cervix with the tenaculum tip. The cervix is fully encircled, and pressure is applied using the colpotomizer. The uterus is pushed towards the opposite shoulder, and the round ligament is coagulated using a ligature, followed by the fallopian tube.

The surgeon uses a ligature to cook and cut the round ligament approximately four centimeters lateral to the uterus on the right side. The ovarian ligament is identified and dissected, followed by the major salpings and major ovarian structures. The procedure then moves to the left side, where the round ligament is similarly cooked and cut. The fallopian tube is held, desiccated, and dissected with the ligature. The blinking ureteric catheter is visible, ensuring the ureter is not damaged. The uterus is pushed to the five o'clock position with a left-hand grasper, stretching the anterior leaf of the broad ligament. The right-hand harmonic is used to push down the advantageous tissue, and the peritoneum is cut from left to right.

The surgeon positions the uterus from the five to seven o'clock position, retroverting it towards the right corner of the sacral promontory. Using a harmonic scalpel, the bladder is separated from the vaginal fascia, which is visible over the colpotomizer. The bladder is pushed laterally and upwards, revealing a crisscross pattern of vessels. The posterior peritoneum is not taken, but coagulation is applied to the ascending uterine branch to prevent reverse bleeding. The uterine artery and vein are coagulated together without cutting.

The surgeon uses the contralateral port to coagulate the uterine artery on the right side, ensuring the blinking ureter is visible to avoid injury. The uterine artery is desiccated and dissected, with initial coagulation followed by cutting to achieve hemostasis. The left side is completed, and the ligature is transferred to the right side for final coagulation and cutting of the vascular pedicle.

The surgeon coagulates and cuts the uterine artery, securing both sides of the vascular bundle. The uterus appears pale and avascular, indicating successful hemostasis. The ureteric stents are visible, illuminating on either side. The surgeon begins colpotomy using the harmonic scalpel, with minimal bleeding observed. The harmonic tip remains within the ring of the colpotomizer during the procedure.

The surgeon performs anterior colpotomy using the tip of the harmonic, carefully cutting around the cervix while the white Teflon colpotomizer is visible. The posterior and lateral colpotomy are completed with minimal energy, preserving the vaginal part of the utero sacral ligament. Any oozing from the vaginal vault is coagulated using bipolar cautery to prevent bleeding after uterus removal. The uterus is now free, and the assistant prepares to extract it.

The surgeon uses a tenaculum to hold the uterus as it is removed. A sponge is packed into the vagina to prevent gas leakage. Anteromedial traction is applied, and the surgeon performs bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy using a ligature or harmonic device, cutting near the ovary. The assistant uses a stone-holding forceps to introduce a needle through the vagina for suturing the uterosacral ligament with a curved needle and vicryl suture.

The assistant holds the peritoneum anteriorly while the surgeon takes continuous suturing bites along the colpotomy wound, which measures approximately 3.5 centimeters. Care is taken to avoid including the bladder in the sutures. The surgeon interlocks the suture at the corner of the utero sacral ligament and anterior vagina, ensuring secure closure. The left hand holds the suture as a retractor, while the right hand continues suturing the vaginal vault, locking each stitch for security. The procedure concludes with a final knot at the opposite corner, ensuring the vaginal vault is securely closed.

The surgeon completes the suturing by tying a surgeon's knot with a double wrap followed by two alternating wraps, ensuring the knot is tightened in the posterior fornix. The assisting needle holder is used to take a careful bite, avoiding the iliac vessel. The final knot is secured, and the suture tails are cut with scissors. The tail of the suture is pulled into the 5 mm cannula, which is then removed along with the needle. The surgical field is inspected, showing minimal blood loss and a well-maintained arc of the uterosacral ligament. The ureteric catheter is visible, and suction irrigation is performed to clean the area.
